chunk_summary: Guidance on managing food refusal, neophobia, and feeding disorder warning signs in children under 2 years, emphasizing structured mealtimes, repeated exposure to new foods, and identifying red flags for further evaluation.
learning_objectives: Understand developmental phases of food refusal and neophobia in children under 2 years.
Learn strategies for structured mealtimes and repeated exposure to new foods.
Identify red flags for feeding disorders requiring further evaluation or referral.
Recognize the importance of monitoring weekly weight trends and overall dietary patterns.
key_facts: Food neophobia is common at 8–12 months and toddlerhood, requiring 8–10 neutral exposures to new foods for acceptance.
Structured meals should include at least one 'safe' food alongside new items, avoiding separate menus.
Toddlers grazing or sipping milk/juice throughout the day may lack hunger at meals.
Red flags include poor weight gain, recurrent choking, persistent vomiting, or mealtimes exceeding 30 minutes.
Extreme selectivity, sensory aversion past 10 months, or developmental delays may indicate feeding disorders.
Conditions like anemia, reflux, or neurodevelopmental issues should be assessed in severe cases.
reference_quote: A new food may need 8–10 neutral offerings on different days before acceptance.
document_key_topics: Food refusal and neophobia in young children
Feeding disorder warning signs and red flags
Developmental and medical considerations in feeding
